title: Quicksort
code: static void QuickSort(int[] a, int lo, int hi)
static int Partition(int[] a, int lo, int hi)
for (int j = lo; j < hi; j++)

frames.caption: QuickSort(a, 0, 5): sort this subarray by partitioning around a pivot.
frames.note: Quicksort sorts a subarray a[lo..hi] in place: pick a pivot, partition so everything smaller sits left of it and everything larger sits right, then recurse into each side. The pivot lands at its FINAL sorted index, so — unlike merge sort — there is no combine step. Average cost is O(n log n) from balanced splits; the only enemy is a consistently bad pivot.

frames.caption: Partition a[0..5]: pivot = a[5] = 4 (last element).
frames.note: Lomuto takes the LAST element as the pivot and uses one boundary pointer i, initialized to lo-1, marking the high-water mark of the "< pivot" region. Production code randomizes which element becomes the pivot first (median-of-three also works) — a FIXED pivot is exactly what makes already-sorted input degrade to O(n squared). Here the pivot is fixed only so the animation is reproducible.

frames.caption: i = lo - 1 = -1: the boundary of the "< 4" region (empty so far).
frames.note: Lomuto takes the LAST element as the pivot and uses one boundary pointer i, initialized to lo-1, marking the high-water mark of the "< pivot" region. Production code randomizes which element becomes the pivot first (median-of-three also works) — a FIXED pivot is exactly what makes already-sorted input degrade to O(n squared). Here the pivot is fixed only so the animation is reproducible.

frames.caption: a[0] = 3 < 4: grow region, i = 0, swap a[0] <-> a[0].
frames.note: a[j] < pivot, so it must join the "< pivot" region. We grow that region by bumping i, then swap a[i] and a[j] to drop this small element just past the boundary. Lomuto can do redundant self-swaps (when i == j), the reason it averages more swaps than Hoare.

frames.caption: a[1] = 6 >= 4: leave it on the right, advance j.
frames.note: a[j] >= pivot, so this element belongs in the "larger" region on the right. We do nothing and let j advance — i stays put, so the "< pivot" boundary does not grow this iteration.

frames.caption: a[2] = 1 < 4: grow region, i = 1, swap a[1] <-> a[2].
frames.note: a[j] < pivot, so it must join the "< pivot" region. We grow that region by bumping i, then swap a[i] and a[j] to drop this small element just past the boundary. Lomuto can do redundant self-swaps (when i == j), the reason it averages more swaps than Hoare.

frames.caption: a[3] = 5 >= 4: leave it on the right, advance j.
frames.note: a[j] >= pivot, so this element belongs in the "larger" region on the right. We do nothing and let j advance — i stays put, so the "< pivot" boundary does not grow this iteration.

frames.caption: a[4] = 2 < 4: grow region, i = 2, swap a[2] <-> a[4].
frames.note: a[j] < pivot, so it must join the "< pivot" region. We grow that region by bumping i, then swap a[i] and a[j] to drop this small element just past the boundary. Lomuto can do redundant self-swaps (when i == j), the reason it averages more swaps than Hoare.

frames.caption: Swap pivot a[5] into a[3] — its final sorted index. Return p = 3.
frames.note: The scan is done: a[lo..i] are all < pivot and a[i+1..hi-1] are all >= pivot. Swapping the pivot from a[hi] into a[i+1] drops it into the gap between the two regions — its TRUE sorted position. We return i+1 so the caller knows where to split.

frames.caption: Pivot fixed at index 3. Recurse LEFT a[0..2], then RIGHT a[4..5].
frames.note: The pivot is now fixed forever at its sorted index, so we never touch it again. Recurse LEFT into a[lo..p-1] and RIGHT into a[p+1..hi] independently. Quicksort is in-place — the only extra memory is the O(log n) average recursion stack (O(n) worst case on degenerate splits).

frames.caption: QuickSort(a, 0, 2): sort this subarray by partitioning around a pivot.
frames.note: Quicksort sorts a subarray a[lo..hi] in place: pick a pivot, partition so everything smaller sits left of it and everything larger sits right, then recurse into each side. The pivot lands at its FINAL sorted index, so — unlike merge sort — there is no combine step. Average cost is O(n log n) from balanced splits; the only enemy is a consistently bad pivot.

Here the pivot is fixed only so the animation is reproducible.

frames.caption: i = lo - 1 = -1: the boundary of the "< 2" region (empty so far).
frames.note: Lomuto takes the LAST element as the pivot and uses one boundary pointer i, initialized to lo-1, marking the high-water mark of the "< pivot" region. Production code randomizes which element becomes the pivot first (median-of-three also works) — a FIXED pivot is exactly what makes already-sorted input degrade to O(n squared). Here the pivot is fixed only so the animation is reproducible.

frames.caption: a[0] = 3 >= 2: leave it on the right, advance j.
frames.note: a[j] >= pivot, so this element belongs in the "larger" region on the right. We do nothing and let j advance — i stays put, so the "< pivot" boundary does not grow this iteration.

frames.caption: a[1] = 1 < 2: grow region, i = 0, swap a[0] <-> a[1].
frames.note: a[j] < pivot, so it must join the "< pivot" region. We grow that region by bumping i, then swap a[i] and a[j] to drop this small element just past the boundary. Lomuto can do redundant self-swaps (when i == j), the reason it averages more swaps than Hoare.

frames.caption: Swap pivot a[2] into a[1] — its final sorted index. Return p = 1.
frames.note: The scan is done: a[lo..i] are all < pivot and a[i+1..hi-1] are all >= pivot. Swapping the pivot from a[hi] into a[i+1] drops it into the gap between the two regions — its TRUE sorted position. We return i+1 so the caller knows where to split.

frames.caption: Pivot fixed at index 1. Recurse LEFT a[0..0], then RIGHT a[2..2].
frames.note: The pivot is now fixed forever at its sorted index, so we never touch it again. Recurse LEFT into a[lo..p-1] and RIGHT into a[p+1..hi] independently. Quicksort is in-place — the only extra memory is the O(log n) average recursion stack (O(n) worst case on degenerate splits).

frames.caption: QuickSort(a, 0, 0): lo >= hi -> subarray of size <= 1 is already sorted.
frames.note: Base case: lo >= hi means the subarray has 0 or 1 element, which is already sorted, so this branch just returns. These trivial returns are the leaves of the recursion tree.

frames.caption: QuickSort(a, 2, 2): lo >= hi -> subarray of size <= 1 is already sorted.
frames.note: Base case: lo >= hi means the subarray has 0 or 1 element, which is already sorted, so this branch just returns. These trivial returns are the leaves of the recursion tree.

frames.caption: QuickSort(a, 4, 5): sort this subarray by partitioning around a pivot.
frames.note: Quicksort sorts a subarray a[lo..hi] in place: pick a pivot, partition so everything smaller sits left of it and everything larger sits right, then recurse into each side. The pivot lands at its FINAL sorted index, so — unlike merge sort — there is no combine step. Average cost is O(n log n) from balanced splits; the only enemy is a consistently bad pivot.

Here the pivot is fixed only so the animation is reproducible.

frames.caption: i = lo - 1 = 3: the boundary of the "< 5" region (empty so far).
frames.note: Lomuto takes the LAST element as the pivot and uses one boundary pointer i, initialized to lo-1, marking the high-water mark of the "< pivot" region. Production code randomizes which element becomes the pivot first (median-of-three also works) — a FIXED pivot is exactly what makes already-sorted input degrade to O(n squared). Here the pivot is fixed only so the animation is reproducible.

frames.caption: a[4] = 6 >= 5: leave it on the right, advance j.
frames.note: a[j] >= pivot, so this element belongs in the "larger" region on the right. We do nothing and let j advance — i stays put, so the "< pivot" boundary does not grow this iteration.

frames.caption: Swap pivot a[5] into a[4] — its final sorted index. Return p = 4.
frames.note: The scan is done: a[lo..i] are all < pivot and a[i+1..hi-1] are all >= pivot. Swapping the pivot from a[hi] into a[i+1] drops it into the gap between the two regions — its TRUE sorted position. We return i+1 so the caller knows where to split.

frames.caption: Pivot fixed at index 4. Recurse LEFT a[4..3], then RIGHT a[5..5].
frames.note: The pivot is now fixed forever at its sorted index, so we never touch it again. Recurse LEFT into a[lo..p-1] and RIGHT into a[p+1..hi] independently. Quicksort is in-place — the only extra memory is the O(log n) average recursion stack (O(n) worst case on degenerate splits).

frames.caption: QuickSort(a, 4, 3): lo >= hi -> subarray of size <= 1 is already sorted.
frames.note: Base case: lo >= hi means the subarray has 0 or 1 element, which is already sorted, so this branch just returns. These trivial returns are the leaves of the recursion tree.

frames.caption: QuickSort(a, 5, 5): lo >= hi -> subarray of size <= 1 is already sorted.
frames.note: Base case: lo >= hi means the subarray has 0 or 1 element, which is already sorted, so this branch just returns. These trivial returns are the leaves of the recursion tree.

frames.caption: Every pivot landed at its sorted index and every subarray hit a base case — the array is sorted.
frames.note: Every partition has placed its pivot, and every subarray has shrunk to a base case — the array is sorted. In place, O(n log n) average, but remember the caveats: NOT stable (long-distance swaps reorder equal keys) and O(n squared) worst case unless the pivot is randomized (introsort caps depth and falls back to heapsort to guarantee O(n log n)).
